question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: isComplete() in PollingContext (PollingTopicMessageRetriever)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a filter with a limit that is exactly equal to retrieverProperties.getMaxPageSize() (default 1000), causing isComplete() to return true when pageSize.get() < maxPageSize is false (pageSize == maxPageSize), prematurely terminating the historical retrieval even if more messages exist beyond the page boundary, and when the live listener then delivers the next message, missingMessages() detects a gap and triggers an unthrottled gap-fill query that may return messages in a different order than the original throttled retrieval?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribe(Topic) in RedisTopicListener] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retryWhen(Retry.backoff(Long.MAX_VALUE, interval).maxBackoff(interval.multipliedBy(4L))) retry logic in RedisTopicListener.subscribe() by causing Redis connection failures (e.g., via connection exhaustion from many concurrent subscriptions), so that during the retry backoff period, messages published to the Redis channel are lost, and when the connection is restored, the subscriber's stream resumes from the live position without the missed messages, creating a permanent gap in the delivered history that is not filled by the gap-recovery mechanism?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter(ConsensusTopicQuery) in ConsensusController] Can an unprivileged external user send a ConsensusTopicQuery with limit=Long.MAX_VALUE (the maximum protobuf int64 value), causing TopicMessageFilter to be built with limit=Long.MAX_VALUE, and since hasLimit() returns true (limit > 0), the flux.take(filter.getLimit()) call attempts to take Long.MAX_VALUE messages, effectively making the subscription infinite while consuming server resources, and the incomingMessages() limit calculation (filter.getLimit() - topicContext.getCount().get()) may overflow to a negative value after enough messages, causing the live listener to be called with a negative limit that bypasses the hasLimit() check?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: missingMessages(TopicContext, TopicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the safety check path (current == null) where gapFilter is built with only startTime set and no endTime or limit, causing topicMessageRetriever.retrieve(gapFilter, false) to run in unthrottled mode with maxPolls=12 and return up to 12 * 5000 = 60,000 messages, and if the topic has more messages than this limit, the gap recovery is incomplete, leaving the subscriber with a history that has a gap at the 60,000-message boundary that is never filled?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter) in PollingTopicListener] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic with a very early startTime and a large limit, causing PollingTopicListener.poll() to repeatedly query the database with pageSize = Math.min(limit, listenerProperties.getMaxPageSize()) (up to 5000 per poll), and since the poll interval has jitter (jitter(0.1)), two consecutive polls may overlap in their time windows if the database is slow, causing duplicate messages to be returned that are then deduplicated by the sequenceNumber filter in subscribeTopic(), silently dropping messages and creating apparent gaps?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: onNext(TopicMessage) in TopicContext] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the non-atomic nature of the TopicContext state update where last.set(topicMessage) and count.incrementAndGet() are two separate operations, so that in a concurrent reactive stream where multiple threads process messages, a thread reading last.get() between these two operations sees the new last but the old count, causing the limit calculation in incomingMessages() (filter.getLimit() - topicContext.getCount().get()) to be off by one, potentially delivering one extra message or terminating one message early?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByFilter(TopicMessageFilter) in TopicMessageRepositoryCustomImpl] Can an unprivileged external user craft a filter with endTime set to a value that is exactly equal to a message's consensusTimestamp, exploiting the strict less-than predicate (cb.lessThan(root.get(CONSENSUS_TIMESTAMP), filter.getEndTime())) to exclude that boundary message from the historical query, while the live listener's filterMessage() check uses >= for startTime but has no endTime check, causing the boundary message to appear in the live stream but not in the historical stream, creating an inconsistency in the delivered history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribeTopic(TopicMessageFilter) in TopicMessageServiceImpl] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequence-number deduplication filter (.filter(t -> last == null || t.getSequenceNumber() > last.getSequenceNumber())) which uses strict greater-than comparison, so that if the live stream delivers a message with sequenceNumber equal to last.getSequenceNumber() (a duplicate), it is silently dropped, but if the historical retriever also returned that message, the subscriber's count is incremented for the historical copy but not the live copy, causing the limit-based termination to fire one message early and truncate the history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getSharedListener(TopicMessageFilter) in RedisTopicListener] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the topicMessages ConcurrentHashMap in RedisTopicListener where computeIfAbsent() creates a shared Flux for each topic channel, and since the shared Flux is created with .share() (which multicasts to all current subscribers), a subscriber that connects after the shared Flux has already emitted some messages will miss those messages and only receive future ones, with the gap-fill mechanism in missingMessages() responsible for backfilling, but if the gap-fill query is slow, the live stream may deliver messages that arrive after the gap-fill completes, causing out-of-order delivery?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse(TopicMessage) in ConsensusController] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic with chunked messages and observe that when t.getInitialTransactionId() is null AND t.getPayerAccountId() is null, the chunkBuilder is built without an initialTransactionID, causing the ConsensusMessageChunkInfo to have no transaction ID, which downstream consumers may interpret as a different chunk ordering than intended, effectively reorganizing the visible sequence of chunked message assembly?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: isValidStartTime() in TopicMessageFilter] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the race condition in isValidStartTime() where the check startTime <= DomainUtils.now() is evaluated at filter construction time, but the actual database query is executed later after the reactive pipeline is assembled, so that a startTime that was valid at construction time may be in the future by the time the query runs (if the server clock is adjusted), causing the retriever to return an empty historical set and the live stream to start from a future position, skipping all messages between the original startTime and the actual query execution time?
